questions.question: What is the purpose of building a personal CBT toolkit?

options.text: To collect as many techniques as possible regardless of usefulness
To identify the specific CBT skills that work best for you and make them easily accessible
To impress others with your knowledge of CBT
To prove you've completed the course

questions.explanation: Correct. Your personal toolkit is the set of skills you've tested and found effective for your specific challenges. Not every CBT technique works equally well for everyone — your toolkit contains what actually helps you, organized so you can access it when you need it.

questions.question: Which factors should guide what you include in your personal CBT toolkit?

options.text: Only include the most complex, advanced techniques
Include skills that: (a) you've practiced, (b) work for your specific symptoms, (c) are realistic to use regularly
Include every technique you've learned about, whether you've used it or not
Only include skills your therapist recommends

questions.explanation: Yes. Your toolkit should be practical and personalized: skills you've actually used (not just read about), that address your symptoms (exposure for anxiety, behavioral activation for depression), and that fit your life (5-minute breathing exercise beats 30-minute meditation if you won't do the latter).

questions.question: Why is it important to document specific examples of CBT skills working for you?

options.text: To prove to others that CBT works
Because when you're distressed, specific memories of past success make skills feel more believable and accessible
Documentation isn't important — just remembering the skill is enough

questions.explanation: Exactly. When you're anxious, your brain says 'CBT won't work this time.' But if your toolkit includes 'Thought record on 3/15: anxiety dropped from 85 to 40 after challenging catastrophizing about presentation,' that concrete evidence makes the skill feel real and worth trying again.

questions.question: How should you organize your CBT toolkit for maximum usefulness?

options.text: By symptom or situation (anxiety → grounding + thought records; low motivation → behavioral activation)
By difficulty level, easiest to hardest
Organization doesn't matter as long as everything is written down

questions.explanation: Correct. Organizing by symptom or trigger makes the toolkit usable in the moment: 'I'm feeling anxious → go to anxiety section → try grounding exercise first, then thought record if needed.' This removes decision-making when you're distressed and increases the chance you'll actually use the skills.

questions.question: Design your personal CBT toolkit. List 3-5 skills that have worked best for you (or seem most promising if you're just starting). For each, note: what symptom/situation it addresses, how to use it (brief steps), and one example of it working (or how you'll test it). Where will you keep this toolkit?
